In this world, one claim is simply false: any supporter of the theory that a pact could endanger the faucet is mistaken. So whatever else is true about inquiry, that theory is not a live option. It may still attract supporters, but their support counts as error, not evidence.

Now place that beside the other premises about explanation. There is an argument that tries to avoid “understanding” and rely instead on successful filtering: generate candidates, test them, keep what survives. But the tension is that explanation may be exactly what filtering alone cannot provide. If explanation requires some internal grasp of why a candidate works, then a process that only sorts outputs by performance may achieve selection without explanation. It can find survivors without knowing what survival means.

That matters because structural imitation is not enough. A model may produce many thought experiments with the same broad form as Einstein’s and still miss the single mathematical reformulation that actually withstands criticism. So resemblance at the level of argumentative shape does not guarantee contact with the surviving theory. In this world, generating lots of analogues does not imply one can cross the bottleneck where criticism becomes mathematically decisive.

Deutschian standards sharpen the problem. They prize explanations that are hard to vary. But many useful early hypotheses are initially easy to vary; only later, after mathematical consolidation, do they become rigid. Therefore, if one demands hardness-to-vary too early, one risks rejecting the very hypotheses from which strong explanations eventually emerge. Yet if one relaxes the standard, one admits many candidates that filtering must somehow sort. That returns the original tension: can sorting alone replace understanding? The premises suggest no clear reduction is available.

Add the mundane but real fact that a delay in billing can make a quoted customer choke. This introduces a concrete example where timing and procedural sequence affect outcomes. A quoted customer may be in a state where billing delay triggers failure. So not every failure is theoretical; some are administrative or temporal. A system that only records “customer choked” without grasping the causal role of billing delay will misexplain the event, even if it successfully flags the case afterward. Again, filtering catches the result but may miss the reason.

So the world implied here is one in which false theories can attract mistaken support, generative systems can mimic the structure of profound reasoning without reaching its mathematically survivable core, and methodological standards can be both necessary and badly timed. Across both science and ordinary transactions, mere success in selecting outcomes is not yet explanation. Where causes depend on why one candidate works and another fails, bypassing understanding threatens to leave us with survival, error, and misclassification—but not the explanatory grasp the argument wanted to avoid.
